parentQuestion: A patient receives an IV infusion of distilled water by mistake. Red blood cells begin to swell and burst. What osmotic process caused this?

options: The ability of a solution to affect cell volume through osmosis
scaffolds.explain: TONICITY is the ability of a solution to affect CELL VOLUME through osmosis. It determines whether cells shrink, swell, or stay the same.

wrongExplains.0: Tonicity affects shape indirectly by changing volume.
wrongExplains.2: Tonicity relates to water movement, not color.
wrongExplains.3: Tonicity affects volume, not division rate.

options: Lower solute concentration than the cell interior
scaffolds.explain: A HYPOTONIC solution has LOWER SOLUTE concentration than the cell interior. Water moves INTO the cell, causing swelling.

wrongExplains.0: Higher solute is hypertonic; hypotonic has less.
wrongExplains.2: Equal solute is isotonic; hypotonic has less.
wrongExplains.3: Hypotonic solutions contain water; they have low solute.

scaffolds.q: What happens to cells in a hypotonic solution?
scaffolds.explain: Cells SWELL as WATER ENTERS when placed in a hypotonic solution. Water follows the concentration gradient into the cell.

wrongExplains.2: Staying the same occurs in isotonic solutions.

options: Red blood cell bursting due to excess water
scaffolds.explain: HEMOLYSIS is RED BLOOD CELL BURSTING due to excess water entry. 'Hemo' = blood, 'lysis' = breaking apart.

wrongExplains.0: Cell shrinkage is crenation; hemolysis is bursting.
wrongExplains.2: Division is mitosis; hemolysis is cell rupture.
wrongExplains.3: Protein synthesis is ribosomal; hemolysis is cell lysis.

scaffolds.q: Is distilled water hypotonic, hypertonic, or isotonic to cells?
scaffolds.explain: Distilled water is HYPOTONIC — it has essentially NO SOLUTES. Water rushes into cells, which have higher solute concentration.

wrongExplains.0: Hypertonic would have high solute; distilled water has none.
wrongExplains.2: Isotonic would match cell concentration; distilled water doesn't.
wrongExplains.3: Distilled water is always hypotonic to cells.

scaffolds.q: Why is it dangerous to infuse pure water IV?
options: The hypotonic solution causes cells to swell and burst
scaffolds.explain: Pure water is HYPOTONIC, causing cells to SWELL AND BURST (hemolysis). This releases hemoglobin and can be fatal.

wrongExplains.0: Temperature isn't the main issue; osmotic damage is.
wrongExplains.2: Distilled water is sterile; the danger is osmotic.
wrongExplains.3: Acidity isn't the issue; osmotic imbalance is.

options: Cell shrinkage in a hypertonic solution
scaffolds.explain: CRENATION is CELL SHRINKAGE in a HYPERTONIC solution. Water leaves the cell, causing it to shrivel.

wrongExplains.0: Swelling occurs in hypotonic solutions; crenation is shrinkage.
wrongExplains.2: Division is mitosis; crenation is shrinkage.
wrongExplains.3: Crenation is shrinkage; severe cases may lead to death.

scaffolds.q: What IV solution is safe for cells?
scaffolds.explain: ISOTONIC SALINE (0.9% NaCl) is safe because it matches cell tonicity. Cells neither swell nor shrink.

wrongExplains.0: Distilled water is hypotonic and causes hemolysis.
wrongExplains.2: Concentrated salt is hypertonic and causes crenation.
wrongExplains.3: Alcohol would damage cells through different mechanisms.

scaffolds.q: A patient receives an IV infusion of distilled water. Red blood cells swell and burst. What osmotic process caused this?
scaffolds.explain: HEMOLYSIS from HYPOTONIC SOLUTION. Distilled water has no solutes, so water rushes into RBCs, causing them to burst.

wrongExplains.0: Crenation is shrinkage; hemolysis is bursting from swelling.
wrongExplains.2: Osmosis definitely occurred — water moved into cells.
wrongExplains.3: This is passive osmosis, not active transport.
